critic_raw: The heart failure readmission prediction design is well-structured. I want to probe the planned readmission exclusion: 'Planned readmissions (scheduled procedures or transfers for specialized care) are excluded via procedure code matching and spot-checked manual review on a validation sample.' Procedure code matching for planned readmissions may miss some planned readmissions that don't have clear procedure codes (e.g., a follow-up admission that becomes an unplanned escalation). If planned readmissions are not perfectly excluded, they would be false negatives (readmission occurred but labeled as non-event). This could affect the model's precision-recall balance.
defender_raw: Planned readmission exclusion via procedure code matching is the standard approach in clinical informatics for this outcome definition (e.g., CMS Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program uses similar criteria). The design adds manual spot-checks on a validation sample to estimate the exclusion accuracy — this quantifies how much residual planned readmission contamination exists. If the spot-check reveals poor exclusion accuracy, the analysis can adjust (e.g., use all readmissions without planned exclusion, or apply a more conservative exclusion criteria). Both models face the same label definition, so any residual contamination applies symmetrically. The 'validate against claims or ED logs where available' provides a cross-reference to improve accuracy.
adjudication_raw: One concern: procedure code matching may incompletely exclude planned readmissions. Addressed: spot-checked manual review quantifies exclusion accuracy; cross-reference with claims/ED logs improves accuracy; both models face same label definition. Verdict: defense_wins.
